I do not think that anyone at this point will assert that the standard was correctly applied with regard to the removal of Eric and Danielle Gandy from Ms. Smith’s home, and in fact the agency itself changed workers and reversed itself.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is the agency decision in New York subject to any kind of judicial review?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: No, the agency decision is subject to the conference that the District Court declared unconstitutional, and then the child may be removed. After the child is removed, there is a full administrative fair hearing provided by Section 400 of the Social Services Law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then that is subject to judicial review.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: And then that is subject to judicial review, perhaps many, many months and years down the road after the child has been subjected to an incorrect decision. After Eric and Danielle, for example, could have been out of Ms. Smith’s home for a year-and-a-half by the time this case could have gotten into State Court to review whether or not Ms. Smith indeed had arthritis that made her incapable of caring for the children.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think the District Court would require that the full trial-type hearing prior to removal be subject to judicial review?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Well, I do not think that they required a full trial-type hearing; but I think it was their intention, I think the opinion specifically says, that they contemplate this to be the final administrative decision. So that judicial review could be obtained immediately, and if any stay was going to be granted that of course would be up to the court; it hardly would be automatic. But I think that they were trying to make this decision-making process as speedy as possible rather than allowing the present procedure, which contemplates a conference first, the removal and then full-scale review after the harm has already been done, so that in a sense the harm is really irrevocable, because the child has already been subjected to a removal, which may have not been necessary and which constitutional standards we believe could require and make much more adequate in terms of making a decision for these children. We are talking about children who are being separated from what may be the only home they know. We're talking --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Ms. Lowry, could I just put this other side of the problem to you for a moment? Supposing the agency adopted a rule that in a single foster-parent situation -- Ms. Smith was a 53-year-old widow. A single parent over 52 or 53 years old suffering from a severe illness of some kind such as arthritis should not in the normal case retain custody of the child such as this. Would such a rule be arbitrary?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: I think it would not be subject to Federal Court jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if that were adopted as agency policy and you impose the hearing, who shall decide whether or not the policy should apply in a he given case? It is going to be an employee of the agency, isn’t it?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: No, the person who would decide whether the decision was arbitrary would be a public official.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, who is this independent decision-maker? That's the question I was getting at. What does the Constitution require with respect to the identity of the person who shall decide whether or not to apply the policy?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: I think that the only thing that the Constitution requires here is that the decision-maker not be previously involved in the decision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But where does the Constitution require that? Why isn’t it perhaps wiser to have a person who has some background with the family and the child and all the rest making the decision?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Well, I think -- first of all, there is nothing to require that this decision-maker not be someone with some background in Social Services. But because --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, but with knowledge of the facts of this particular case. Why does that disqualify a person otherwise independent, having no financial interest, no interest other than the wellbeing of the child?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Because the people who then appear before the decision-maker who want to present the information to the decision-maker are burdened by the fact that the decision-maker has knowledge of a great deal of information that the other people may not know of and may not have an opportunity to refute. If the Social Services’ official has been involved in the decision, as the record shows many of them were, then they may have access to a great deal of information that, in fact, is not accurate; and when the family and the child’s representative or the child comes in to explain why the decision is incorrect, then the people who come in to explain that will not know about what information the decision-maker has in his or her head, and they can't refute it. That is why it is important the decision-maker make a decision based on information that's presented then that each side can have an opportunity to refute. This doesn't require lawyers. It can be as informal as people sitting down and talking. But the decision cannot be made fairly unless all parties know what the other side has to say and have an opportunity to say, "This fact is wrong, but this fact is right" or "The fact is half-right and half-wrong".

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Ms. Lowry, I am still in trouble. Is this person a person outside of the agency?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: The person is an employee of the Social Service district, and if the child is in care with the Social Service district --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I mean, I'm talking about the one you want. Will you settle for that?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I think we are talking about the same kind of decision-maker as in Goldberg.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The only one you want is somebody that didn’t handle this particular case.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: We want someone who was not involved in this particular case who will make a decision based on the information presented to them at the time everybody has an opportunity to hear the information.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, is the man and woman at the conference such a person?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: At the conference itself.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: At the conference, no. The person at the conference, the record shows, usually was involved in the decision-making at the initial stages.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you would settle if it was another equivalent person -- for example, somebody had another authorized case, but didn't have this one.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: That is right, but we would --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You'd be satisfied.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Yes, but we would say that it cannot be someone from the private agency; it has to be a public official, as the conference now requires. These are private agencies that are publicly funded and are delegated with the responsibility of taking care of these children. It is a public function, and we would say that an in-agency conference would not be adequate; but a Social Service agency conference just as was contemplated in Goldberg v. Kelly would be adequate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you don't want to overhaul the whole Social Services.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: No, Your Honor, not at all. We are asking only for the same kind of decision-maker as is presently provided in the revised City procedure. We are not asking for a different kind of decision-maker.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, all these arguments go to what kind of piece of legislation the State of New York ought to have dealing with this sensitive subject.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I believe not. The District Court said that the exact details of the procedure should be worked out by New York; but the question of whether or not there should be a procedure and whether or not it should satisfy minimal due process with the right of everyone to be heard I believe is a Federal issue and is a constitutional issue. And I believe that these children have been condemned to suffer grievous loss, they do have a constitutionality protected interest in the relationship, in this family relationship, which may be their only family relationship and their only hope for permanence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what I can't understand is that they are going to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Who are you representing, the interests of the children or the foster parents?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: I am here representing the interests of the foster family. As you know, Your Honor, I represent the foster parents. They have an interest in the foster-family relationship.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you were just speaking about the children’s rights. I thought your friend was representing their interests and the State's.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Well, it is our position that we represent the foster children’s rights as articulated by the District Court. Ms. Buttenwieser, as you know, has not requested such relief; the foster parents have requested such relief, and we believe we have standing to raise the children’s interests with regard to this right. Mr. Justice Rehnquist, I'm sorry, I did not hear your question.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: You had a problem common on many counsel, being peppered by more than one member of the Court at a time. What is the issue that this hearing is supposed to resolve?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Alright, with regard to the most common situation, 80% of the situations in which the child is being removed from the foster home and to be transferred someplace else. The hearing is to decide whether the transfer is in the child’s best interests.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: And is the reason that that is because New York says that is what the issue is?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: New York says that children can be removed at the agency’s discretion. I believe that New York also takes as a matter of legislative policy the decisions made with regard to children should be made in their best interests.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: But now, is this question to be answered at the hearing one that has its direct source in New York law, or is it superimposed as part of the constitutional guarantee by the District Court?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: No, the District Court did not --

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Which?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: I'm sorry. The District Court did not deal with standards at all. The District Court was very explicit, the questions to be answered at the hearing about what' in the best interests of the children are a matter solely of State policy. There is not Federal policy that is imposed by the District Court decision. The District Court says that the hearing is only to adequately effectuate State policy and that it is constitutionally required, but to effectuate State policy and not to impose any kinds of Federal policy. The question to be answered when the child is going to be returned to a natural parent is the question that is detailed by State law, it is a-State law standard that has to be applied then, and it is a different standard than when the child is going to go to another foster family; but it is determined by State law.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: And each of these standards is now applied by various New York public agencies, but just not in the same manner as the District Court thinks they ought to be applied.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: They are not applied in a constitutionally adequate manner, and the decision is not reviewed in any kind of a constitutionally required manner until after the child is out of the home. It is reviewed after the child is out of the home.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Ms. Lowry, could I ask another question? The foster parents' status as having authority to receive foster children is something that's renewed periodically, is it?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Yes, it is.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does the child have any right to protest the withdrawal of a license of a foster home?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: The child does not under New York law, but that would be -- that would likely --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This could happen, could it not, for example, if they might adopt a rule that no one over 50 years of age with arthritis can be a foster parent?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: The State could do that, that's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And without any notice or a hearing to a child who might be affected by such a decision.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Well, I think that if a child were in a foster home and there was such a relationship, then the fact that the license was going to be withdrawn would be an issue at the hearing; and I believe under the District Court decision that would require a hearing, and I believe even under the existing procedures. There is presently a procedure where that foster parent could challenge the determination of withdrawal of the license; but that would be again after the child is removed. Section 400, I believe, contemplates that; but that again is after the harm has been done, the unnecessary harm.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the logic of the District Court’s opinion would seem to require that the child whose liberty was going to be deprived by the laws of license by the foster family would be the party interested in such a hearing.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Constitutionally.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Yes and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And he or she would have a right to procedural due process accorded by the Fourteenth Amendment before New York could cancel a license of a foster parent...

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Yes, and I believe --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: His or her foster parent.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: That's right. I believe that's a situation covered by the District Court’s order, because in that case the reason for the removal would be the cancellation of the license, and then there would be a hearing at which it could be determined whether it was in the best interests of the child then to be removed from that foster home.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Ms. Lowry, suppose the District Court in another case says that before the child surrendered by its own parents can be accepted by the State, there must be a hearing. Would you complain about that?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Not at all, Your Honor. In fact --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: You would want that.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: We would want that.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, I mean, in other words, you're saying that nothing can be done concerning the child without a hearing.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: No, we're not saying nothing can be done without a --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, can the foster parent paddle the child without a hearing?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: Yes, as long as it's not excessive.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: That doesn't have anything to do with liberty at all, does it?

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I don't think it does; I think that that becomes a State-law issue whether --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: (Inaudible) I didn't hear it.

turns.name: MARCIA ROBINSON LOWRY
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I think that the only time a hearing is required is when there is going to be a substantial deprivation to the children, and we think that children should not come into foster care unnecessarily. We think that the best thing for a child is to stay with the natural family under most circumstances; we have no quarrel with that. All we're saying is that after time has passed and after a child has been in the relationship for a year or more, the child then has an interest in the relationship and has an interest in not being subjected to arbitrary deprivation of that relationship. We're not talking at all about substantive standards when we're talking about the natural family. Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: Very well. Ms. Marcus?

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: Your Honor, let me just point to the record to correct a few errors that were made in appellees' argument. So far as the statistics are concerned, Page 90(a) of the Appendix contains a projection based on '75 and early '76 figures, which state that as to New York City there would 4,200 removals or changes of status. Half of those would be children discharged from foster care so that we've got half being transferred within, half being discharged out. Now, in figures by the Children’s Child Welfare Information Service, which is a computerized service that keeps records on this for the agencies and for the City. 1975 figures were attached to interveners' reply brief, and that showed that of the discharges -- and now, we are talking about half of transfers, half are within the system, half out -- of the half that goes out. In the year 1975 of the group of children in foster care for more than a year, 48.1% returned to the natural parents. So those are the statistical facts as shown by the record.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what about appellees' argument that there is no liberty interest or that if there is one all the process that is due is being afforded?

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: Both, Your Honor, there can't be a liberty interest, because if there were one it would collide head-on with the one already declared by this Court to the natural family, first for the reason that I stated before, that the standard has got to change. If we're going to have --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does this case only involve cases where the natural parent wants the children back?

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: No, but the point is that once you declare a liberty interest in the foster-care relationship, it is going to change the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what if a natural parent isn't involved and it's just the State has decided to change the custody?

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: The State cannot guarantee a liberty interest in a relationship out of which one party can walk at any time. The natural parents --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, would you say anybody could complain at all if New York decided, well, we've been giving too much due process here. We are going to discontinue foster care at the discretion of the agencies without any hearing, without any notice, without any sit-down discussion, without any trial-type hearing at all, and not subject to any judicial review whatsoever. We're just going to transfer the children.

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: Well, that would abrogate 392, of course.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I just said let's assume New York decides to do it.

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: That decides to abrogate existing statutes. I think New York is not under any constitutional obligation to provide any foster-care services. This is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that isn't what I asked you. They are providing it, and they are going to make, say, 2,000 transfers a year in New York City from one foster family to another and without any hearing, without any notice, without explaining anything at all and without any judicial review. Now, you would say they could do that, because there is no liberty interest whatsoever.

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: Yes, I would say that although as a matter of policy, obviously that would be undesirable and would not be done under New York law. You cannot have a liberty interest in a relationship in which one party has no obligation whatever to remain with it. Imagine what would happen if a natural parent or an adoptive parent decided to walk out of a relationship. They would be jailed, and they would be publicly stigmatized. There is a great difference between an adoptive and natural parent and the foster parent here, there is no obligation. Ms. Goldberg was obligated only so long as she took the State’s money.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would you think that State could without any notice whatsoever and without any hearing terminate the parents’ rights once --

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: The natural parents’ rights?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is because why?

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: Because this Court has declared that there is a constitutional right for a natural family to be together, and under this Court's decision it would not --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think the child's --

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: The decisions would not be possible to abrogate the rights of a natural family.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think the child himself would have an interest in that interest? Do you think the child would have standing?

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: Well, as a member of the natural family, he would partake of that interest; but that does not occur here. And I might add as to the second part of your question --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you agree that the child certainly has a liberty interest in remaining with his natural family.

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: Yes, this Court has so declared and we are under this Court’s mandate. Now insofar as --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But not with respect to remaining with any foster family.

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: Foster family because of the complete difference in that kind of relationship.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose the parents are dead, and the State has custody of the child.

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: The State always has custody in the case where the child is in foster care, and then of course the foster parents by statute have a mandatory statutory preference to become the adoptive parents in that situation

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, does anyone have standing -- would anybody have any kind of standing whatsoever or any interest that you would recognize to question the State's decision in terminating a particular foster parent?

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: Well, I want to correct also a misconception about 392, which came up in appellees’ argument, 392 of the Social Services Law. The foster parent can ask the court to maintain that child in his home. It does operate as a possible pre-removal judicial hearing, and there are a number of cases cited in our brief where that was done. It does not have to wait until years and years later. This is a pre-removal judicial hearing which is available.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And in what court?

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: And that is in the Family Court of the State of New York.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's not the United States District Court.

turns.name: MARIA L. MARCUS
turns.text: No, this is in the Family Court of the State of New York, and of course with usual appeals and eventually to this Court if there's something amiss. Now, Justice Stevens brought up the question of whether the decision below contemplated the child having some role, the decision below didn't say so. But under 392, this judicial proceeding that we have under the Family Court Act, the child is there, and it's presumed that he will be because there's a statutory section in that, 392(6), which says that the court may dispense with the presence of the child. So it obviously is contemplated that the child will be there and will be heard unless the court feels that it's not necessary.

turns.name: WARREN E. BURGER
turns.text: Your time has expired, Ms. Marcus. Thank you ladies. The case is submitted.
